en.extract:
The rufous-breasted wood quail (Odontophorus speciosus) is a species of bird in the family Odontophoridae. It is found in Bolivia, Ecuador, and Peru primarily on the east side of the Andes between 800 and 2000m in elevation (higher in Peru).
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forests.
It has been listed as least concern.


Feeding
The Rufous-breasted wood quail eats insects and nuts especially in summer, as well as plants. Unlike many other bird species, they require a high protein diet. 


Subspecies
Source:

O. s. soderstromii Lönnberg & Rendahl, 1922 – extreme S Colombia (SE Nariño) and E & S Ecuador.
O. s. speciosus Tschudi, 1843 – N & C Peru (Amazonas S to Ayacucho).
O. s. loricatus Todd, 1932 – SE Peru (Cuzco) to Bolivia (E to Santa Cruz).


References

The '''rufous-breasted wood quail''' ('''''Odontophorus speciosus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Odontophoridae]]. It is found in [[Bolivia]], [[Ecuador]], and [[Peru]] primarily on the east side of the [[Andes]] between 800 and 2000m in elevation (higher in Peru).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/Species-Account/nb/species/rbwqua1/overview|title=Rufous-breasted Wood-Quail - Introduction {{!}} Neotropical Birds Online|website=neotropical.birds.cornell.edu|access-date=2019-05-30}}</ref>

Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist [[Montane forest|montane forests]].

It has been listed as least concern.

== Feeding ==
The Rufous-breasted wood quail eats insects and nuts especially in summer, as well as plants. Unlike many other bird species, they require a high protein diet. 

== Subspecies ==
Source:<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/rufous-breasted-wood-quail-odontophorus-speciosus|title=Rufous-breasted Wood-quail (Odontophorus speciosus)|website=www.hbw.com|language=en|access-date=2019-05-30}}</ref>
* ''O. s. soderstromii'' Lönnberg & Rendahl, 1922 – extreme S Colombia (SE Nariño) and E & S Ecuador.
* ''O. s. speciosus'' Tschudi, 1843 – N & C Peru (Amazonas S to Ayacucho).
* ''O. s. loricatus'' Todd, 1932 – SE Peru (Cuzco) to Bolivia (E to Santa Cruz).
